post_2: small2015stabilitycorenets the article by mario small and colleagues conducted various studies and had incredible discoveries. for instance, the researchers discovered how one's routine helps develop new experiences and has an impact on whom one turns to for support. along with this, they saw how college students respond to new obligations and how ongoing routines add to the evidence that context matters to the structure of social support and networks. something i thought was interesting was how people respond quickly to changing circumstances and adapt by changing their discussion partners. the core discussion network is a major source of support throughout a person’s life, and it represents one of the most important ways that social networks are said to benefit everyday well-being. i would have assumed those who are not fond of change, would have more trouble adjusting to a new routine in their life. in a sense this is true, yet, when they have a social and reliable network, they can adapt more to the changing environment. something that i was a bit confused about was the concept of ego. for instance, ego-centrismism can be presented in various ways. however, does this indicate at a certain age we experience this? would this also be the case for children or elders perhaps? this ties in with the reading of last week by cohen and colleagues, as we see how a change in our social environment (specifically if it is during a stressful situation, like moving out), can affect our health if we don't have a strong support system during a time of need. perkinsetal2015 i liked this article by perkins, as it had an interesting perspective on the social networks in low- and middle-income countries (lmics). the article emphasized the formation of social ties, network structures, and function, and the associated mechanisms linking health or health behaviors, and how this may be relevant in the limited resources or targeting public health and economic development interventions in lmics. however, they were able to find how individuals in communities worldwide are linked together somehow through strong and weak ties representing many types of relationships. i was interested in the results of the interventions, as they can spread more positive than negative outcomes among individuals and communities in lmics. for instance, when they mentioned how a study of 19 slums in nepal demonstrated that an intervention providing access to savings accounts changed the network of financial transactions between two waves of data collection, i was surprised. we become stress-free of the financial burden when interventions allow individuals to make connections and can encourage the type of resources needed among a community that is suffering under these or various conditions. i was also interested in how the article mentioned the effects of hiv in a social context and how it was higher among people who were involved with incoming visitors. i was a bit confused with the concept of ‘change agent’, and wished the article elaborated on this more. this article ties in with the interventions in cohen’s work of week 3. after reading this article, i can now see how interventions can be provided efficiently in lmic communities, which serves as an influence for durkheim’s work with individuals who have experienced or thought of self-harming themselves.

post_1: what is one way in which these specific readings challenged your pre-existing beliefs about ios? what did you believe before versus what you read here? reading afghanistan´s significance for nato strategy by henrik larsen has challenged my pre-existing beliefs about the implication/importance of nato forces when it comes to building relationships with local communities. on page 45, larsen writes “it was hubris to expect young soldiers with deployments of 6–12 months, and with no language skills and little cultural understanding, to win the hearts and minds of afghan elders and to help build local governance and the rule of law”. my prior understanding of army forces was limited to combat operations. therefore, i had never appreciated the significance of building relationships with local communities. now, i know that military forces have a broader role to play in conflict zones beyond combat operations in order to achieve long-term stability and security in a territory. in conclusion, this reading has made me reconsider my assumptions about the role of army forces and the importance of a comprehensive, multi-dimensional approach that includes not only military operations, but also political, economic, and social measures aimed at addressing the underlying causes of instability. this can be something as simple as speaking to people in their own language.
post_2: this chapter was a very interesting and eye-opening read. something that greatly stood out to me throughout the reading was the talk of bias in relation to various categories, including race, gender, and socioeconomic status. the text describes bias as either intentional or unintentional, and it really made me reflect on my own biases. growing up in a homogeneous environment, i didn't realize how much my surroundings shaped my perceptions and judgments. however, despite my thinking that i am unbiased, i now recognize that unconscious bias can significantly affect my judgments and actions. due to this, i will be sure to work on becoming more aware of my biases and actively challenging them. this brings me to my question: do you think that travel and diverse experiences can effectively reduce bias, or is it more complex than that?
